comment_text.sn41.5:1.1: Kāmabhū discussed Dhamma with Ānanda at Ghosita’s Monastery near Kosambī in SN 35.233.
comment_text.sn41.5:4.3: At Ud 7.5:6.1, upon seeing the enlightened monk Bhaddiya the Dwarf.
comment_text.sn41.5:5.1: Nelaṅga: of “faultless” (na ela) “parts” (aṅga). | Ela is unusual, perhaps unique, in early Pali. It corresponds to Sanskrit enas, “fault, offense, sin” (Rig Veda 5.3.7; Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 14.1.1.26). Thus the primary sense of the word is an ethical fault, and it is secondarily applied to the chariot’s parts. | The “parts” of a chariot are discussed at MN 58:10.3. Ethics is a “part” of the spiritual life (AN 4.196:3.1).
comment_text.sn41.5:5.2: A white canopy was the height of luxurious style (SN 45.4:1.4), like the white parasol held over a prince (Snp 3.11:11.3, AN 3.39:1.5, MN 123:20.2).
comment_text.sn41.5:5.3: Perhaps because mindfulness is always useful (SN 46.53:15.4), or because it leads to unification (MN 10:2.1).
comment_text.sn41.5:5.4: In the original setting, Bhaddiya is seen approaching and thus inspiring the whole verse. The phrase “going forward and coming back” is often used of a mendicant’s deportment, especially when entering the village for alms-round.
comment_text.sn41.5:5.5: Kaṭha Upaniṣad 3.3 says the mind is the charioteer, and the body is the chariot (śarīraṁ ratham eva). This is part of a series of very Buddhist-sounding verses about how when one lacks understanding and mindfulness the senses will be out of control. The image is also used by the Buddha at DN 16:2.25.11.
comment_text.sn41.5:5.6: The Pali anīgha is elsewhere only found in negative. It is sometimes traced to Sanskrit ṛghā, “propensity to violence” (Jamison/Brereton “mettle”). Here, however, the non-negative form is nīgha, casting doubt on that etymology. Sanskrit nīgha does not seem to occur (except as a Buddhist back-formation from anīgha), but we do find nighna in the sense “smiting, striking” (Rig Veda 1.55.5; Atharva Veda 10.1.27). The commentary explains it as “suffering”. “Trouble” and its negative “untroubled” seem to more-or-less fit the Pali cases, although perhaps something like “uncombative” might be more precise.

root_text.sn41.5:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā kāmabhū macchikāsaṇḍe viharati ambāṭakavane.
root_text.sn41.5:1.2: Atha kho citto gahapati yenāyasmā kāmabhū tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ kāmabhuṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho cittaṁ gahapatiṁ āyasmā kāmabhū etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.5:4.1: Imassa nu kho, gahapati, saṅkhittena bhāsitassa kathaṁ vitthārena attho daṭṭhabbo”ti?
root_text.sn41.5:4.2: “Kiṁ nu kho etaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā bhāsitan”ti?
root_text.sn41.5:4.4: “Tena hi, bhante, muhuttaṁ āgamehi yāvassa atthaṁ pekkhāmī”ti.
root_text.sn41.5:4.5: Atha kho citto gahapati muhuttaṁ tuṇhī hutvā āyasmantaṁ kāmabhuṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.5:5.5: ‘Ratho’ti kho, bhante, imassetaṁ cātumahābhūtikassa kāyassa adhivacanaṁ mātāpettikasambhavassa odanakummāsūpacayassa aniccucchādanaparimaddanabhedanaviddhaṁsanadhammassa.
root_text.sn41.5:5.6: Rāgo kho, bhante, nīgho, doso nīgho, moho nīgho.
root_text.sn41.5:5.7: Te khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.5:5.11: Sā khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.5:5.13: Rāgo kho, bhante, bandhanaṁ, doso bandhanaṁ, moho bandhanaṁ.
root_text.sn41.5:5.14: Te khīṇāsavassa bhikkhuno pahīnā ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.sn41.5:5.16: Iti kho, bhante, yaṁ taṁ bhagavatā vuttaṁ:
root_text.sn41.5:7.1: Imassa kho, bhante, bhagavatā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa evaṁ vitthārena atthaṁ ājānāmī”ti.
root_text.sn41.5:7.3: Yassa te gambhīre buddhavacane paññācakkhu kamatī”ti.

translation_text.sn41.5:1.1: At one time Venerable Kāmabhū was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove.
translation_text.sn41.5:1.2: Then Citta the householder went up to Venerable Kāmabhū, bowed, and sat down to one side. Kāmabhū said to him,
translation_text.sn41.5:4.1: How should we see the detailed meaning of this brief statement?”
translation_text.sn41.5:4.2: “Sir, was this spoken by the Buddha?”
translation_text.sn41.5:4.4: “Well then, sir, please wait a while as I consider the meaning of this.”
translation_text.sn41.5:4.5: Then after staying silent for a while, Citta said to Kāmabhū:
translation_text.sn41.5:5.1: “Sir, ‘faultless parts’ is a term for ethics.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.2: ‘White canopy’ is a term for freedom.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.3: ‘One spoke’ is a term for mindfulness.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.4: ‘Rolls on’ is a term for going forward and coming back.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.5: ‘Chariot’ is a term for this body made up of the four principal states, produced by mother and father, built up from rice and porridge, liable to impermanence, to wearing away and erosion, to breaking up and destruction.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.6: Greed, hate, and delusion are troubles.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.7: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.8: That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is called ‘untroubled’.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.9: ‘The one who comes’ is a term for the perfected one.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.11: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given this up, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, and obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.12: That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is said to have ‘cut the stream’.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.13: Greed, hate, and delusion are bonds.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.14: A mendicant who has ended the defilements has given these up, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, and obliterated them, so they are unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.15: That’s why a mendicant who has ended the defilements is called ‘unbound’.
translation_text.sn41.5:5.16: So, sir, that’s how I understand the detailed meaning of what the Buddha said in brief:
translation_text.sn41.5:7.3: to traverse the Buddha’s deep teachings with the eye of wisdom.”
